The [2000–01 season](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Football_League "2000–01 Football League") was [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.")'s 94th season in [the Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") and the seventh in the [third tier](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division").[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

Manager [Gudjon Thordarson](/wiki/Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Guðjón Þórðarson") again brought in some of his fellow countrymen as Stoke were looking to gain promotion to the First Division. The close season saw Stoke beat higher placed teams in the form of [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") but a goalless draw with [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") emphasized that pre-season results mean nothing. Stoke's form was steady if not spectacular but two terrible cup exists, defeat to [Nuneaton Borough](/wiki/Nuneaton_Town_F.C. "Nuneaton Town F.C.") in the FA Cup and a shocking 8–0 loss at home to Liverpool in the League Cup threatened to derail Stoke's season. But Stoke responded by going 12 matches unbeaten and for the second season running Stoke entered the lottery of the play-offs. [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") were the opponents and after a 0–0 draw the "Saddlers" proved to be too strong at the [Bescot Stadium](/wiki/Bescot_Stadium "Bescot Stadium") and won the match 4–2 leaving Stoke facing a fourth season in the third tier.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

## Season review

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Season review")]

### League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: League")]

The Icelandic invasion of [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent") was now in full flow with no less than ten Scandinavian players agreeing to join the club.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) Amongst them were club record signing [Brynjar Gunnarsson](/wiki/Brynjar_Gunnarsson "Brynjar Gunnarsson"), the manager's son [Bjarni Guðjónsson](/wiki/Bjarni_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Bjarni Guðjónsson"), Swedish full-back [Mikael Hansson](/wiki/Mikael_Hansson "Mikael Hansson") and new forwards [Stefán Þórðarson](/wiki/Stef%C3%A1n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Stefán Þórðarson") and [Ríkharður Daðason](/wiki/R%C3%ADkhar%C3%B0ur_Da%C3%B0ason "Ríkharður Daðason") although Daðason took until October before he could complete his move from Norwegian football.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) Stoke went on a pre-season tour of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") and then had two impressive results against [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) [Tony Dorigo](/wiki/Tony_Dorigo "Tony Dorigo") and [Wayne Thomas](/wiki/Wayne_Thomas_(footballer,_born_1979) "Wayne Thomas (footballer, born 1979)") both joined the side before the season opener at home to [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") with excitement rife after the good pre-season.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) But a frustrating 0–0 followed and those it seemed that pre-season results are pretty pointless.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

Stoke soon started to get their act together and began to find the back of the net regularly with [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C.") beaten 3–0 and [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") 4–0 and a 3–3 draw away at [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.").[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) The first [Potteries derby](/wiki/Potteries_derby "Potteries derby") ended 1–1 but in the next match against [Rotherham United](/wiki/Rotherham_United_F.C. "Rotherham United F.C.") on-loan striker [Marvin Robinson](/wiki/Marvin_Robinson_(footballer) "Marvin Robinson (footballer)") broke his leg in a nasty collision with the Rotherham goalkeeper.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) A last minute victory over [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") was a vital win against a promotion rival and with the arrival of Dadason it seemed that Stoke could continue their push for an automatic position but two truly awful defeats in the FA and League Cup saw the feeling around the club drop and after a 3–1 defeat at home to bottom of the table [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C.") there was a major danger that those results could affect their league form.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) But City to their credit got their act together and went on a 13 match unbeaten run cementing their place in the play-offs.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

But Stoke not making life easy for themselves lost to several lowly placed sides increasing the risk of being caught but three wins at the end of the season saw Stoke finish in 5th position with 77 points and they come up against an impressive [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") side. The first leg at the Britannia was a tense affair which saw [Ben Petty](/wiki/Ben_Petty "Ben Petty") 'sacrifice' himself to prevent a goal for Walsall and the match ended 0–0.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1) The second leg started well enough for Stoke with [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") putting City into the lead but a goalkeeping howler from [Gavin Ward](/wiki/Gavin_Ward_(footballer) "Gavin Ward (footballer)") gifted the "Saddlers" an equaliser. In a mad 15 minute spell in the second half Walsall scored three without reply and despite [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") scoring it was too little to late and Stoke would have to try again to gain that promotion out of the third tier.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

### FA Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: FA Cup")]

Stoke were on the receiving end of a cup shock as after a 0–0 draw at home with non-league [Nuneaton Borough](/wiki/Nuneaton_Town_F.C. "Nuneaton Town F.C."), the replay was a stereotypical cup upset waiting to happen with heavy rain a muddy pitch and a hostile home crowd. And it all proved too scripted for Stoke as in the last minute Marc McGregor secured a famous win for Nuneaton.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

### League Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: League Cup")]

Stoke had a decent run to the fourth round after beating [York City](/wiki/York_City_F.C. "York City F.C.") 5–1, Premier League [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") on away goals after a thrilling 5–5 aggregate score and a 3–2 over [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") saw Stoke earn a glamour tie against [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). But it quickly turned into a nightmare for Stoke as Liverpool ran riot scoring with every shot they took and ended up with an 8–0 victory, Stoke's worst home defeat.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)

### League Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: League Trophy")]

As holders Stoke were looking to retain the [Football League Trophy](/wiki/Football_League_Trophy "Football League Trophy") and they made it to the northern section semi final against local rivals [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.") after beating [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough_F.C. "Scarborough F.C.") (3–1), [Halifax Town](/wiki/Halifax_Town_A.F.C. "Halifax Town A.F.C.") (3–2) and [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") (4–0). The tie was originally scheduled to be played at [Vale Park](/wiki/Vale_Park "Vale Park") but with several failed attempts to play the match due to a waterlogged pitch the match was moved to the [Britannia Stadium](/wiki/Britannia_Stadium "Britannia Stadium"). Vale won a dour match 2–1 after extra time and they went on to lift the trophy.[[1]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-1)
